text: Water inflow and outburst events are common geological hazards in tunnel engineering, particularly the sudden inflow of water carrying a significant amount of debris, which poses the greatest risks. Water inflow and outbursts frequently occur in areas with dense joint and fracture zones or weathered and fractured zones formed by tectonics. Sudden inflow disasters predominantly happen in karst caves, regions with developed dissolution cavities, and at the interfaces between aquifers and aquitards. According to statistics, 80% of tunnels completed in our country have encountered sudden inflow hazards during construction, with 31 of them experiencing total water inflow rates exceeding 10,000 m³/d. The Dayaoshan Tunnel on the Jingguang Line encountered an inflow of 30,000 m³/d while crossing the No. 9 fault, and its shaft was also flooded due to sudden inflow, resulting in significant losses. The Shamulada Tunnel on the Chengkun Line (6.383 km) experienced multiple events of sudden inflow, with a maximum rate of 52,000 m³/d, resulting in a 32-day work stoppage and severe water leakage post-opening, leading to remediation costs nearing 10 million yuan over several years. Internationally, the old Tanna Tunnel in Japan (7.804 km), which commenced construction in 1918, faced major sudden inflows six times, with water pressures ranging from 1.4 to 4.2 MPa. The largest influx from a major fault reached 288,000 m³/d, and the total inflow upon completion was 14,515,200 m³/d, delaying the tunnel's completion until 1934. The Kiyomizu Tunnel (9.70 km) encountered an inflow of 15,840 m³/d, while the Daqing Tunnel (22.22 km) experienced a sudden inflow of 120,384 m³/d. The Seikan Tunnel (53.850 km) faced sudden inflows of 115,200 m³/d on four occasions, resulting in 34 fatalities and over 1,300 injuries, with control taking more than five months, and a construction period delayed by ten years compared to the original schedule. The Simplon Tunnel (19.800 km) between Switzerland and Italy had to expend five times the planned budget for controlling mountain pressure and groundwater, resulting in a delay of one and a half years. In the Dayaoshan Tunnel on the Hengguang Line, a sudden water inflow and sand eruption occurred in the middle section at a distance of two kilometers, with a maximum outflow rate of 50,000 m³/d, flooding the shaft and forcing construction to halt.

Water inrush is one of the most significant geological hazards during tunnel construction, second only to collapses, with sudden water inrush and the influx of large quantities of debris posing the greatest threats. Research indicates that adverse geological conditions conducive to water inrush disasters primarily include faults, large karst caves and underground rivers, goaf areas (old water accumulation) in coal-bearing strata, as well as old water accumulation in metallic and non-metallic mines. Statistics show that prior to 1988, 80% of tunnels constructed in China encountered sudden water inrush disasters during their construction, with total inflow rates exceeding 10,000 m³/d in 31 cases. The Shamulada Tunnel on the Chengdu-Kunming Railway (6.383 km long) experienced multiple instances of sudden water inrush, with a maximum inflow rate reaching 52,000 m³/d, resulting in a 32-day work stoppage and severe leakage post-commissioning, with remediation costs nearing 10 million yuan over several years. The Nanling Tunnel of the Jingguang Line, measuring 6.06 km in length, reported a total inflow of 19,550 m³/d; during the construction of the Jiabamboo Tunnel on the Nankun Railway, numerous instances of karst conduit water inrush occurred within the tunnel, with an inflow rate reaching 80,000 m³/d. As the water inrush occurred at the high portal end, it could not naturally drain towards the portal, resulting in tunnel inundation and work stoppage. The Caoyuan Tunnel, a single-line tunnel on the Weishe-Kunming section of the Nankun Line, experienced a significant increase in groundwater, leading to substantial water inrush along with collapses, with a maximum inflow rate of 6,500 m³/d. Internationally, Japan's Shimizu Tunnel (9.70 km) encountered a water inrush of 15,840 m³/d; the Daqingshui Tunnel (22.22 km) experienced a water inrush of 120,384 m³/d; and the Seikan Tunnel (53.850 km) encountered sudden water inrushes four times, peaking at 115,200 m³/d, leading to 34 fatalities and over 1,300 injuries, with control taking more than five months and an overall delay of ten years beyond the planned construction schedule.

Tunnels, as significant structures, are widely utilized in highway engineering in the "three high and four active" southwestern region. However, during construction in extremely complex geological environments, they inevitably encounter typical underground engineering geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, large deformations, and water inrushes, which severely constrain safe and efficient construction, increasing economic costs and management pressures. Conducting risk assessments for tunnel geological hazards and implementing effective emergency response measures are of vital importance for disaster prevention and mitigation in tunnel projects. 

Based on research on the engineering geological conditions of the southwestern mountainous area, this paper analyzes the types and characteristics of geological hazards encountered during the construction of highway tunnels in this region. On this basis, the Mudashan Tunnel, a representative deeply buried long tunnel in the southwestern mountainous area, is selected as the research subject. The geological hazards during the construction process of this tunnel are identified, and the risk categories are determined. Subsequently, a risk evaluation index system for geological hazards in tunnel construction is established, and the weight of the indices is determined. A risk evaluation model based on index scoring methods is constructed, and this model is used to evaluate the geological hazard risks of the Mudashan Tunnel. Finally, emergency response measures for geological hazards in mountainous highway tunnels are proposed.

The main conclusions of this paper are as follows: 
(1) The southwestern region is located at the southeastern edge of the Eurasian Plate, adjacent to the Pacific Plate and the Indian Plate. Significant active faults are developed within the area, with strong neotectonic movements and frequent seismic activities, which are structurally controlled and lead to the formation of longitudinal canyon landforms. Most engineering structures traversing this area are tunnels. The extremely complex geological environment is a core issue faced during tunnel construction in the southwestern mountainous region and is also an intrinsic factor that causes collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations in tunnel construction. 

(2) The Mudashan Tunnel is situated at the junction of the Songpan-Ganzi orogenic belt and the western edge of the Yangtze Platform, crossing 17 major faults, with engineering geological characteristics such as significant burial depth, substantial lithological variation, complex geological structures, and developed groundwater. These geological characteristics result in a high risk level for water inrushes and large deformations, with high risk levels for collapses and rock bursts in the construction of the Mudashan Tunnel.

(3) Based on the risk characteristics of highway tunnels in the southwestern mountainous area, a risk assessment method for tunnels in this region is established, namely the index scoring method. The evaluation index system of the index scoring method comprises 18 scoring indicators categorized into geological factors, design factors, and construction factors. By determining the indicator weights, this method is applied to assess the risks of large deformations, collapses, water inrushes, and rock bursts in the Mudashan Tunnel, yielding evaluation conclusions that correspond to the types and levels of hazards that occurred during its construction. This indicates that the method is applicable for safety risk assessment of mountain highway tunnel construction.

(4) Based on clarifying the mechanisms and manifestation characteristics of collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations, the phases of emergency rescue for tunnel engineering geological hazards are divided into: preparation phase, implementation phase, and rescue summary phase. In conjunction with the entire process of sudden incidents—pre-event, event, during-event, and post-event—emergency response ideas and a categorized and graded emergency management mechanism are proposed for preemptive prevention, emergency rescue, and later disposal.

(5) For sudden geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, and water inrushes, it is proposed that the preemptive prevention phase adhere to the principle of "investigate before excavation." For gradual geological hazards characterized by large deformations, emphasis is placed on emergency preparedness, utilizing diverse monitoring means, and timely warnings based on monitoring results.

The phenomenon of sudden mud and water inflow, or sudden water flow, occurs during the construction of underground chambers or tunnels when passing through areas with developed karst caves, especially when encountering underground river systems, thick layers of gravel-bearing aquifers, and large fractured zones connected to surface water (see Figure 2-19). This poses a significant hazard to the construction of tunnels and other underground works. Sudden water influx events can be roughly categorized into three types based on geological conditions: structural sudden inflow, exposed sudden inflow, and contact-zone sudden inflow. Among these, structural sudden inflow includes sudden inflows from fractured zones, fissures, and karst underground rivers; exposed sudden inflow comprises sudden inflows from loose gravel layers, confined aquifer exposures, and exposed karst structures. 

During tunnel construction, signs of imminent water inflow can be identified through several distinct indicators, primarily including: falling rock or ceiling collapse in the tunnel face; expansion or cracking of the impermeable rock layers along the tunnel excavation path; wetting of rocks in the inflow area, leading to dripping water with a gradually increasing volume; water, mud, or sandflow emerging from cracks or boreholes at the tunnel face; a "hissing" sound generated by the friction between rocks as high-pressure water from the aquifer or water accumulation area is forced into the fissures; and an increase in harmful gas concentrations at the tunnel face, including the release of methane, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen sulfide from the water accumulation area.

Factors influencing water ingress: Topography and landform: The topography and landforms affect the recharge, flow, and discharge of groundwater. A substantial transfer of surface water underground increases the groundwater level, which is one of the primary causes of tunnel water ingress disasters. Groundwater: Groundwater influences the occurrence of water ingress through static and dynamic pressure, both of which can lead to hydraulic fracturing of rocks, increasing the degree of fissure opening, enhancing permeability, and compromising local aquicludes, resulting in elevated groundwater levels that trigger water ingress events. The groundwater recharge conditions significantly impact the quantity and duration of water ingress; the development of surface runoff and connectivity with surrounding water sources can enhance groundwater recharge. Lithology of strata: The impact of different lithologies on water ingress disasters varies. Higher permeability rocks are more prone to leakage; soluble rocks, such as limestone and dolomite, yield significantly greater tunnel water ingress. Hard rock layers, such as limestone and sandstone, often exhibit strong aquifer properties, making them susceptible to forming water-rich fault zones in areas with abundant water resources, leading to water ingress incidents. Conversely, soft rock layers such as shale and mudstone tend to become muddy and soften upon contact with water, becoming major sources of water ingress disasters. Tunnel length and depth: The longer the tunnel, the larger the area for water collection and recharge, resulting in an increased total volume of water ingress. As the tunnel depth increases, the hydraulic head of the groundwater will also rise, resulting in a higher volume of water ingress per unit time.

At the Mohei end of the Dafuya Pass Tunnel, the long-term groundwater level is below the dry line. The crown of the tunnel in the uphill line is 128 m above the highest groundwater level, while the crown of the tunnel in the downhill line is 24 m above the highest groundwater level. The abundance of groundwater is the primary reason for the considerable presence of groundwater within the tunnel, compounded by Yunnan being a rainy province. Prolonged heavy rainfall leads to saturation of the mountain soils and excess precipitation infiltrating deeply through the fractures in the strata, draining out from the mountain's vulnerable segments, one of which is the tunnel itself. The mountainous area where the tunnel is located originally had two irrigation channels built by local farmers; there was a continuous flow of water at the onset of tunneling. However, after advancing 400 m into the tunnel, both irrigation channels have dried up. This indicates that the water in the channels has flowed out of the tunnel along the fractures in the rock. It is evident that due to the influence of the Ailao Mountain Fault and secondary structures on the Dafuya Pass Tunnel, the strata exhibit well-developed fractures, allowing surface water to easily enter the tunnel, significantly impacting tunnel construction. From a geological perspective, the accumulation of water flow is controlled by strata layers or through-going joint planes. The excavation direction at the Mohei end is NE50°, while the rock layers in the tunnel trend NW5° to 10°. During excavation, new strata typically first appear on the right side of the tunnel, and most of the inflowing water also arises from the right side and the top. The volume of inflow changes with lithological variations. The occurrence of inflow is also related to lithology; in sandstone, water typically flows in a linear fashion, while in slate, water generally seeps from multiple points simultaneously, and in limestone, water typically appears in linear or vein-like forms. Since lithological interfaces are often water-rich areas, locations where lithology changes are also prone to inflow. Further geological structural studies of the Dafuya Pass Tunnel indicate that the zones of relative structural compression and relaxation occur intermittently at equal intervals. The structural compression zones exhibit strong tectonic compression phenomena, with steeply inclined rock layers, developed schistosity on both sides of rock veins, and no significant open joint planes; quartz or calcite veins develop and the rock mass is dry. In contrast, the structural relaxation zones have rock layer inclinations consistent with the regional stratification, with open joint planes, mostly unfilled, and the rock mass is humid or exhibiting seepage and even inflow. Almost all sections of collapse and inflow in the tunnel are located within the structural relaxation zones.

Tunnel construction water inrush includes inflow during tunnel excavation and sudden water inrush. Inflow during tunnel excavation refers to the water intrusion resulting from the excavation penetrating the inundation zones within the tunnel cross-section due to the exertion of gravitational stress, causing water from these disaster structures to flow into the excavated tunnel space. Sudden water inrush during tunnel construction refers to water from disaster structures existing a certain distance ahead of the tunnel face or outside the excavation profile, where under gravitational stress, water breaches the geological barrier between the disaster structure and the tunnel excavation face (tunnel face, excavation profile) and flows into the excavated tunnel space; or due to rainfall in the area where the tunnel is located, causing rapid groundwater level rise in the disaster structure, increasing stress on the impermeable geological barrier, leading to water breaching this barrier and flowing into the excavated tunnel space; or due to blasting vibrations at the tunnel face during construction, causing sudden settlement of water in the disaster structure, which results in the water breaching the geological barrier between the disaster structure and the tunnel excavation face (tunnel face, excavation profile) and flowing into the excavated tunnel space.

Inrush and water burst are common geological hazard issues encountered during tunnel construction. Inrush can carry a substantial amount of debris and often occurs simultaneously with water burst in areas where tunnel joints and fractures are densely packed. Water bursts frequently arise at the interfaces of karst caves and developed dissolution voids, as well as at the boundaries of aquifers and aquicludes. Geological hazards such as water bursts and inrushes during tunnel construction can be managed through drainage, blockage methods, or a combination of both techniques. In addition to addressing water bursts and inrushes, it is essential to monitor and preemptively control the water burst locations of nearby underground rivers and karst caves. Monitoring and preemptive control facilitate geological forecasting during the construction phase. This work should accurately analyze the intersection locations of karst caves, underground rivers, and the tunnel. In the event of water bursts and inrushes, drainage trenches and borehole dewatering measures should be implemented for tunnels that are not deeply buried in non-karst rock. For karst tunnels and shallow-buried tunnels, the primary approach should focus on blocking to the greatest extent possible to prevent groundwater level decline, thus avoiding ground subsidence and well depletion, which can directly damage the surrounding ecological environment. During construction, utilizing an aquiclude prior to excavating the aquifer can effectively prevent geological hazards from water bursts. Moreover, techniques such as advanced drainage and pre-injection grouting can also significantly reduce the severity of water burst geological hazards.

Various geological hazards encountered during tunnel construction not only seriously impact the technical level of construction but also hinder the sustainable development of transportation. Among many geological hazards, the probability of water inrush events is the highest, and the damage caused is the most severe. Water inrush primarily occurs when the original geological structure of the area where the tunnel is located is altered or when the excavation disrupts existing conditions, leading to the drainage of normal groundwater through the tunnel—this constitutes a geological hazard with diverse manifestations, such as substantial sudden sand and silt flow during water inrush; cracks from fractured rock strata result in severe leakage, causing pipeline water or karst fissure water to burst forth. Water inrush refers to a geological hazard involving a massive inflow of water that occurs when tunneling passes through areas with developed voids, thick aquifer gravel layers, or significant fractured zones connected to surface water, also known as catastrophic water inrush. To mitigate the occurrence of water inrush and sudden water events, it is essential to strengthen research and explore effective control measures. In conjunction with addressing water inrush, enhanced monitoring and pre-control should be implemented during tunnel construction. Measures to address sudden water inflows at locations of lost voids and underground rivers near construction sites include both drainage and sealing techniques to facilitate geological forecasting during construction phases. Specifically: (1) To effectively prevent water inrush, an initial isolation layer may be constructed before excavating through the aquifer layer. Additionally, advanced grouting and drainage techniques can be employed to reduce the severity of water inrush geological hazards, primarily focusing on sealing while preventing groundwater level decrease to the maximum extent. (2) Control measures should also be taken for shallow-buried tunnels and lava tunnels. This approach effectively avoids phenomena like spring drying and ground subsidence, thus protecting the surrounding ecological environment and enhancing construction safety. Accurate analysis and pre-control efforts should identify the intersections of caverns, underground rivers, and tunnels. (3) Control measures for non-lava deep-buried tunnels involve applying drainage and excavation tunneling techniques at locations experiencing water inrush and groundwater inflows during tunnel construction.

During tunnel construction, various geological hazards can significantly impact construction levels and directly affect the development of the transportation industry. Currently, water inflow is a common occurrence among geological hazards, which can lead to numerous detrimental effects. Water inflow is influenced by changes in the original geological structure, thereby directly impacting overall tunnel engineering and triggering disasters. Given the diverse types of geological hazards, it is crucial to emphasize effective management of water inflow issues to ensure construction quality. Water inflow is a fairly common geological hazard during tunnel construction that directly affects the work. Therefore, construction personnel need to adopt effective methods to address problems related to mud in rock layers, karst formations, and underground rivers. The issue of water inflow during tunnel construction is prevalent, requiring the use of scientific methods to control drainage and prevent water ingress, thereby minimizing its impact on the construction process. Additionally, it is necessary to establish appropriate alert systems to notify construction personnel about water inflow hazards, enabling timely measures for control. Groundwater accumulation within the tunnel can be effectively managed using enlarged advance holes for drainage, significantly reducing its impacts and better ensuring the success of the tunnel project.

The analysis of the causes of sudden water and mud in the Junchang Tunnel is as follows: (1) The surrounding rock of the tunnel has high weathering and low strength, with strong water-conducting capacity. The rock mass in the sudden water and mud segment consists of completely weathered and strongly weathered granite, with uneven local weathering, severe differential weathering, and contains a small amount of highly weathered fragments; it has a high sand content, reaching up to 80% in some areas, with poor cementation, severely cemented upon contact with water; at the same time, joints and fractures are developed, the rock mass is fragmented, and local areas are extremely broken, resulting in very poor stability of the surrounding rock, with essentially no self-supporting capability. (2) Concentrated rainfall leads to abundant water supply. The water supply is mainly from atmospheric precipitation. The tunnel site area falls under the monsoon humid climate of the subtropical mountainous region, characterized by significant seasonal rainfall, with ample rainfall and high intensity during the rainy season. The site area consists of highly water-rich strata, and the source of sudden water primarily comes from pore water and fracture water in loose rock types. (3) The burial depth is relatively shallow, and the surface water accumulation capacity is strong. The water and mud burst section runs beneath Cencheng Town's Shankou Village, with a relatively shallow burial depth. Geomorphologically, it forms a long, narrow small basin (approximately 2900m long from east to west, and about 400m at the widest point from north to south), relatively flat in the middle with higher surrounding areas, providing a strong topographic condition for water accumulation. The accumulation area is extensive, with developed regional channels and abundant hydraulic recharge. The groundwater flow and the inferred differential weathering boundary are illustrated in Figure 36. (4) There exists a water-conducting fault zone, and the water-conducting passage is developed. Comprehensive surface investigations, tunnel exposures, and disaster analysis indicate that there is a water-conducting fault located nearly perpendicular to the tunnel axis in front of the sudden water and mud point, with a width exceeding 100m.

Disaster-causing structures are the intrinsic conditions and controlling factors for the occurrence of sudden water and mud inrush disasters. These structures possess both the natural attribute of groundwater accumulation and the social attribute of susceptibility to sudden water and mud inrush disasters, representing a complex interaction between geological conditions and underground engineering activities. Through a statistical analysis of 221 cases of sudden water and mud inrush disasters, these disaster-causing structures have been classified into three categories with eleven types: karst types (dissolution fracture type, cave and cavity type, conduit and subterranean river type), fault types (water-rich fault type, water-conducting fault type, water-blocking fault type), and other causative types (intrusive contact type, interlayer fracture type, unconformable contact type, differential weathering type, special condition type). The study investigates the structural characteristics, storage patterns, and geological identification methods of different types of disaster-causing structures and conducts analyses of typical cases. Statistical analysis indicates that karst-type disaster-causing structures are predominant in number (approximately 48%, 105 cases), followed by fault types (approximately 29%, 65 cases), and lastly, other causative types (approximately 23%, 51 cases). Four typical tunnel sudden water and mud inrush disaster patterns are proposed: direct exposure type, progressive failure type, permeability instability type, and intermittent failure type. The research findings establish a foundation for the study of the disaster mechanism and control measures for sudden water and mud inrush disasters, providing reference and guidance for tunnel construction safety.

Water inrush disaster refers to a geological hazard phenomenon where a large volume of water or a slurry mixture suddenly flows into a tunnel during the construction of tunnels and underground works, along structural surfaces such as rock joints, faults, and other adverse geological structures like karst conduits and underground rivers. Based on the mode of water inrush, it can be classified into instantaneous water inrush, stable inflow, and seasonal water inrush types; according to the source type of water inrush disaster, it can be divided into fissure-type inrush, fault-type inrush, cavity collapse-type inrush, and conduit and underground river-type inrush; based on water-resistant and mud-retaining structures, it can be categorized into water-resistant bedrock fracture inrush and filling structure instability inrush, the former primarily reflecting hydrodynamic fracturing under dynamic disturbances and high seepage pressure conditions, while the latter can be subdivided into local seepage instability and overall sliding instability.

Tunnel water inrush disasters consist of three components: (1) The disaster source, which is the driving force, fundamentally involving bodies of water, accumulations, and voids within a specific space. (2) The water inrush channel, which serves as the primary transport pathway for the disaster source, is a mixed body composed of groundwater, mud, and sand with distinct energy storage characteristics. The disaster source is a coupling evolution transmission pathway for the onset of water inrush disasters. The water inrush channel is a necessary condition for the occurrence of surge water disasters. (3) The water-resistant and mud-retaining structure is the final barrier to the disaster source entering the tunnel, namely the dynamic destruction process induced at the ultimate water inrush breach within the structure, where the ruptured inrush is triggered by the combined dynamics of the downhill disaster source’s movement and the disturbance caused by excavation at the face.

Karst water inrush is a significant challenge in the construction of underground engineering projects. The sudden influx of water disaster is often the result of multiple contributing factors, fundamentally rooted in the complexity of the hazard source. Generally, the sources of karst water inrush disasters include voids in underground structures, solid-phase fillings (such as sediments and boulders), and pressurized water bodies within the cavities, often associated with geological structures like fault fracture zones, interlayer slip zones, or unconformity contacts. Solid-phase fillings often collapse due to voids, karst conduits, etc., while sediments and clastic rock materials are transported into the karst caves by groundwater movement, leading to a high degree of uncertainty compared to unfilled karst cavities. 

Typically, water and mud inrush disasters consist of water supply channels, hazard sources, and impermeable rock masses. The evolution process of these disasters is exceptionally complex, involving a dynamic failure process characterized by the disruption of the hydraulic structure, hydrodynamic system, and mechanical equilibrium states of the tunnel surrounding rock due to excavation disturbances. The energy stored in the pressurized water bodies is released rapidly, and solid-phase fillings surge towards the excavated face in a multiphase flow, posing a significant threat to the safe construction of the tunnel. Under normal circumstances, poorly consolidated geological bodies like water-filled caves maintain a relatively stable state without any anthropogenic interference, and their hazard sources do not pose any risk during tunnel construction. However, once the stable state during tunnel excavation undergoes abrupt changes, the hazard of water inrush transforms into a disaster structure of water flooding and mud inrush.

The sudden water inrush in mining voids is a catastrophic dynamic hazard and is a primary type of water inrush in mines. The actual mining environment is often characterized by irregular and complex spatial structures, making it challenging to establish accurate mechanical models for calculating water inrush in mining voids. Therefore, effective monitoring of water inrush in mining voids is particularly important. Currently, monitoring of water inrush in mines mainly relies on predictions based on the stress, strain, pore pressure, and anomalies in electromagnetic characteristics in the inrush area. 

Water inrush in mining voids can be categorized into two types: roof-type and floor-type inrushes. Roof-type water inrushes include those caused by the movement and compression of the roof exerting pressure on accumulated water in the mining void, as well as roof movement or structural activation that connects to the overlying aquifer or accumulated water in the mining void. Floor-type water inrushes involve depressurization failure of the floor or structural activation that connects to the underlying aquifer beneath the floor. 

Regardless of whether the inrush is induced by the failure or movement of the roof or floor, or by structural activation leading to water inrush in the mining void, the process of forming the inrush pathway is associated with stress and energy release, which produces microseismic activity. Furthermore, there is a developmental change in microseismic activity prior to the occurrence of water inrush events. The characteristics of microseismic activity during this process can serve as precursor information for water inrush hazards in mining voids. Through case studies on the characteristics of microseismic precursors in relation to water inrush in mining voids, we can establish a foundation for monitoring precursors related to microseismic activities associated with water inrush.

The hydrological and geological conditions along the Three Gorges Dam Bypass Expressway are extremely complex, with well-developed karst and poor geological formations, resulting in a high risk of water ingress during tunnel construction. To mitigate the risk of sudden water inflow, a high-risk karst tunnel water ingress prevention and control system has been proposed based on extensive engineering practice related to the Three Gorges Dam Bypass Expressway. 

Firstly, through karst hydrological and geological survey methodologies, the lithology, topography, and landforms of the strata are analyzed, revealing the regional karst development characteristics and recharge and drainage pathways, alongside long-term monitoring of rainfall and water flow data to determine the recharge and drainage characteristics of the regional karst water. 

Secondly, by analyzing the risk environment and risk factors associated with sudden water inflow, and integrating dynamic information from the construction engineering, a dynamic risk assessment of tunnel water ingress is conducted, leading to the development and successful implementation of a forward geological prediction system based on tunnel construction risk assessment. 

Finally, based on the results of the comprehensive forward geological prediction, a dynamic optimization design of the water ingress control scheme is performed, identifying the treatment sections and strategies, alongside quality control of excavation, support, and grouting during the construction process. The application of this system in the typical karst tunnel, Jigongling Tunnel, along the Three Gorges Dam Bypass Expressway has proven its effectiveness in avoiding large-scale sudden water inflow geological hazards. This system can serve as a reference for other projects in the Three Gorges Reservoir area and the Qingjiang River basin, demonstrating broad applicability and engineering practicality.

To investigate the evolution process of flooding and mud-inrush disasters caused by tunnel excavation disturbances in fault zones, a three-dimensional geological model testing system was established. Taking the F2 fault water and mud inrush disasters at the Yonglian Tunnel in Jiangxi as an example, new similar materials suitable for fluid-solid coupling model tests were developed through extensive material ratio and physical-mechanical performance testing. This research focuses on the flooding and mud-inrush disasters in tunnels. 

The test results effectively reveal the temporal response patterns of key parameters such as surrounding rock displacement, seepage pressure, stress-strain relationships, and the mass of protrusions in tunnels located within fault fractured zones under unsupported conditions. As time progresses, the overall seepage pressure shows an increasing trend, with greater fluctuations and amplitude closer to the excavation face. The mass of protrusions decreases briefly prior to the occurrence of flooding and mud-inrush disasters, followed by a rapid increase. The surrounding rock arch experiences primarily vertical displacement at the crown, while the arch waist predominantly exhibits horizontal displacement; under the same corresponding stress conditions, the strain at the arch waist is greater than that at the crown. 

A comparative analysis between the experimental disaster characteristics and the actual field evolution process shows a good correlation between the two results. This system can be widely applied to model testing research in other underground engineering projects, and the methodologies and findings have significant guiding and reference value for similar engineering studies.

Through the example analysis of the Jijiapo Tunnel lining fracture water burst project, this study investigates the karst development characteristics in the tunnel site area, proposes a high-angle filling type karst fracture aquifer structure, and analyzes its morphology, filling material properties, and disaster mechanisms. Treatment measures for water hazards in the tunnel lining fracture water burst are employed, including fracture sealing, peripheral grouting, drainage and pressure relief, structural replacement, and evaluation of grouting effectiveness through information technology. The high-angle filling type karst fracture aquifer structure manifests as high-angle fissures, which easily accumulate water and generate high water pressure after rainfall. Additionally, due to its poor permeability and slow sediment transport rate, it is prone to sediment accumulation at its base, where the filling medium solidifies and cements into hard or semi-hard states under the influence of self-weight stress and high water pressure, resulting in poor permeability. Initially, upon excavation exposure, water may not emerge or only a small amount may flow, leading to potential oversight; however, after a period of exposure, the filling medium in the fractures gradually erodes, eventually causing the filling material to produce piping, soil flow, or be completely expelled, subsequently directing water from subterranean rivers, dissolution cavities, or surface water into the tunnel, leading to lining fracture and tunnel water burst disasters. The research findings have not only been successfully applied to the water burst disaster treatment of the Jijiapo Tunnel but also provide significant reference for preventing similar water burst incidents and disaster management.

To study the sudden water influx disaster patterns in fault fracture zone rock masses, experimental research was conducted on the creep-erosion coupled mechanical characteristics of fractured rock masses. An innovative experimental method for water injection around the inner wall of a permeable cylinder and axial stress loading was developed, addressing the core issue of the radial migration of fine materials from the periphery to the center under permeation conditions. Coupled flow-solid tests were conducted under multiple factors to investigate the deformation and seepage characteristics of fractured rock masses during the process of sudden water influx with variable mass. The experimental results indicate that the process of sudden water influx can be divided into three stages: the initial stage, the erosion-dominating stage, and the creep-dominating stage. Among these, the erosion-dominating stage is crucial in determining the onset of disasters due to sudden water influx, while the deformations of the rock mass during the creep-dominating stage can lead to instability hazards in the surrounding rock. In samples with higher axial pressure and larger inner diameter but lower water pressure and bond strength, more significant creep effects can be observed; conversely, in samples with higher water pressure and inner diameter but lower axial pressure and bond strength, more pronounced erosion effects are evident. Axial pressure and bond strength are the main controlling factors for the creep of fractured rock masses; all four factors—axial pressure, water pressure, bond strength, and inner diameter—significantly influence the erosion of the rock mass.

In response to the recurrent water inrush disasters at the Xianxin Tunnel in Guangxi, a comprehensive investigation was conducted using field exploration, laboratory testing, and theoretical analysis methods to understand the mechanism, disaster characteristics, and mitigation strategies of sudden water inrush in the fully weathered granite tunnel. The results indicate that the water inrush disaster at the Xianxin Tunnel exhibits a high degree of suddenness, rapid disaster evolution, severe secondary disasters, and a large impact range. The environmental factors conducive to the occurrence of water inrush include poor stability of the stratum water, well-developed drainage pathways, abundant ground and surface water, and negative topography of the valley catchment. Insufficient grouting implementation, inadequate thickness of the protective rock mass, and construction disturbances are the primary disaster-causing factors for tunnel water inrush. Under the combined action of construction disturbances and groundwater pressure, the instability of seepage at the face leads to the formation and rapid expansion of water inrush pathways, subsequently resulting in secondary disasters such as surface collapse and drying of wells. Considering the safety of tunnel construction and the ecological environment, a comprehensive treatment principle focused on sealing, combined drainage-blockage, segmented control, and dynamic construction was established. The collaborative management technology system was optimized to include composite curtain grouting, full-section curtain grouting, and dynamic construction, which is applied to the mitigation practices for the water inrush disaster in the fully weathered granite section of the Xianxin Tunnel.
